title: Arrhythmia — Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia
oneLiner: Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm; heart pumps no blood; immediate defibrillation is life-saving; seen in ischaemia, cardiomyopathy, electrolyte disorders.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Fine VF masked as asystole:** if rhythm looks like asystole but patient has shockable rhythm → check gain, rotate leads; single finding: any fine undulation (especially in perpendicular lead) means shock, not no-shock
- **Pulseless VT mistaken for unstable VT with pulse:** defibrillating a perfusing rhythm causes cardiac arrest → single finding: confirm **no carotid/femoral pulse** before delivering shock (check for 10 seconds max)
sections.Presentation:
- Collapse / sudden loss of consciousness
- No palpable central pulse (carotid/femoral)
- No respiratory effort or agonal gasps
- Monitor/defib pads show wide-complex tachycardia (QRS >120 ms) with no pulse
- May have brief seizure-like movements (agonal gasps, not true seizure)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Ventricular fibrillation (VF):** chaotic, no discernible QRS; same treatment (shock) → no immediate differentiator needed
- **Asystole:** flatline, no electrical activity; differentiate by checking gain, lead placement, and looking for fine VF waves
- **Pulseless electrical activity (PEA):** organised rhythm (narrow or wide) but no pulse; treat with adrenaline, not shock
- **Supraventricular tachycardia (rarely pulseless):** narrow QRS (<120 ms) with no pulse → more likely PEA, not shockable
- **Splitter:** **Wide QRS (>120 ms) + no pulse = shockable** (VT/VF). Narrow QRS + no pulse = non-shockable (PEA/asystole)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Immediate defibrillator/monitor rhythm strip
  - Confirm pulselessness (carotid/femoral) for ≤10 seconds
  - Capnography waveform (ETCO2) if intubated – low <10 mmHg suggests poor CPR quality, but not diagnostic of rhythm
- **Bloods:**
  - Post-ROSC: ABG/VBG (lactate, pH, pCO2), serum K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, troponin, FBC, coagulation screen
  - Pre-ROSC: none delay defibrillation
- **Imaging:**
  - Post-ROSC: portable CXR (line/tube placement, pneumothorax), bedside echo (focused cardiac ultrasound for tamponade, LV function, wall motion if ROSC)
- **Special tests:** None in acute arrest
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - ALS algorithm – shockable (pVT/VF) vs non-shockable (asystole/PEA) based on rhythm + pulse
- **Never-miss test:** **Rhythm check on monitor/pads** AND **confirm absence of pulse** – omission can lead to shocking a perfusing rhythm (causing arrest) or withholding shocks from a shockable rhythm
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Follow ALS/cardiac arrest algorithm (Resuscitation Council UK)
  - Defibrillation: 150–200 J biphasic, 360 J monophasic – as soon as shockable rhythm identified, charge while compressions ongoing, give shock immediately after pause
  - Immediately resume CPR 30:2 for 2 minutes before next rhythm check
  - Give **adrenaline 1 mg IV** [check local policy for timing – typically after 2nd or 3rd shock] then every 3–5 minutes
  - If refractory after 3 shocks → **amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus** (diluted in 20 mL D5W given fast) then 150 mg if recurrence
  - Manage reversible causes (4 Hs: hypoxia, hypovolaemia, hypo/hyperkalaemia/metabolic, hypothermia; 4 Ts: tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis – coronary or pulmonary)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - If ROSC achieved → transfer to ICU / coronary care unit
  - Targeted temperature management (32–36°C for 24h)
  - Coronary angiography (urgent if STEMI suspected; all presumed ischaemic)
  - Treat underlying cause: electrolyte correction, revascularisation, ICD therapy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Secondary prevention: implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) if no reversible cause (NICE TA95)
  - Assess left ventricular function (echo before discharge)
  - Medication: beta-blocker, ACEi, statin if ischaemic
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A for arrest itself; post-ROSC → ICU orders per local protocol
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Adrenaline 1 mg (1:10 000 solution) IV push every 3–5 minutes during arrest** – can give via IO if no IV access
- **Amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus** (dilute to 20 mL with glucose 5%) for refractory shockable rhythm; may give second dose **150 mg** IV if recurrent
- **Alternatives:** Lidocaine 100 mg IV (if amiodarone unavailable) – but amiodarone is first-line in UK
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** None in arrest (do not delay); amiodarone – caution in pre-existing thyroid disease (post-ROSC). Avoid concurrent QT-prolonging drugs (not immediate concern)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose change needed in arrest. Post-ROSC: amiodarone metabolised by liver – reduce if severe hepatic impairment [check local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving amiodarone to a patient with unstable VT who has a pulse but is NOT in cardiac arrest** – can cause hypotension and precipitate arrest. Use synchronized cardioversion instead. Also: **delaying adrenaline until after first shock** is correct (not before)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Failure to achieve ROSC
  - Post-arrest syndrome (myocardial stunning, systemic inflammation, brain injury)
  - Aspiration, rib fractures, pneumothorax from CPR
  - Amiodarone: hypotension (especially if given fast)
- **Chronic:**
  - Hypoxic brain injury (range: full recovery to persistent vegetative state)
  - Heart failure if myocardial infarction caused arrest
  - Need for ICD with its own complications (inappropriate shocks, infection)
sections.Prognosis: - Survival to hospital discharge for out-of-hospital shockable arrest ~20–30% if bystander CPR and early defibrillation; in-hospital witnessed arrest ~50%. Key predictors: time to defibrillation, initial rhythm, arrest cause (ischaemic vs non-ischaemic)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Minimise pre-shock pause** – charge while compressions continue; give shock within 5 seconds of stopping chest compressions
- **Capnography waveform** – after ROSC, sudden rise in ETCO2 to >40 mmHg is a good sign
- **Consider reversible causes** aloud during arrest (e.g., hyperkalaemia, toxin – give calcium gluconate, sodium bicarbonate per local protocols; tension pneumothorax – needle decompression)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do NOT delay defibrillation for IV access, intubation, or drug administration – shock first
- Do NOT give adrenaline before first shock (if local algorithm says after 2nd shock, wait)
- Do NOT stop chest compressions for longer than 10 seconds at any point (rhythm check, pulse check, shock)
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- Rare – most patients are not discharged directly from arrest. If discharged home after ROSC and ICD placement:
  - **Return if:** any shock from ICD (especially multiple or inappropriate), syncope, palpitations, chest pain
  - **Follow-up:** electrophysiology clinic within 4–6 weeks, cardiac rehabilitation, echo at 3 months
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call crash team** immediately on any pulseless, unresponsive patient (cardiac arrest call)
- **Post-ROSC escalation to ICU:** any ROSC >2 minutes, GCS <15, hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg despite fluids/vasopressors), lactate >4 mmol/L, or need for respiratory support
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 >7 (if not in arrest), sustained VF/pVT after 3 shocks → ask for senior anaesthetist/ICU consultant, consider alternative defibrillation (e.g., double sequential defibrillation per local protocol)

rawMarkdown:
## Arrhythmia — Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia
**One-liner:** Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm; heart pumps no blood; immediate defibrillation is life-saving; seen in ischaemia, cardiomyopathy, electrolyte disorders.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Fine VF masked as asystole:** if rhythm looks like asystole but patient has shockable rhythm → check gain, rotate leads; single finding: any fine undulation (especially in perpendicular lead) means shock, not no-shock
- **Pulseless VT mistaken for unstable VT with pulse:** defibrillating a perfusing rhythm causes cardiac arrest → single finding: confirm **no carotid/femoral pulse** before delivering shock (check for 10 seconds max)

### Presentation
- Collapse / sudden loss of consciousness
- No palpable central pulse (carotid/femoral)
- No respiratory effort or agonal gasps
- Monitor/defib pads show wide-complex tachycardia (QRS >120 ms) with no pulse
- May have brief seizure-like movements (agonal gasps, not true seizure)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Ventricular fibrillation (VF):** chaotic, no discernible QRS; same treatment (shock) → no immediate differentiator needed
- **Asystole:** flatline, no electrical activity; differentiate by checking gain, lead placement, and looking for fine VF waves
- **Pulseless electrical activity (PEA):** organised rhythm (narrow or wide) but no pulse; treat with adrenaline, not shock
- **Supraventricular tachycardia (rarely pulseless):** narrow QRS (<120 ms) with no pulse → more likely PEA, not shockable
- **Splitter:** **Wide QRS (>120 ms) + no pulse = shockable** (VT/VF). Narrow QRS + no pulse = non-shockable (PEA/asystole)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Immediate defibrillator/monitor rhythm strip
  - Confirm pulselessness (carotid/femoral) for ≤10 seconds
  - Capnography waveform (ETCO2) if intubated – low <10 mmHg suggests poor CPR quality, but not diagnostic of rhythm
- **Bloods:**
  - Post-ROSC: ABG/VBG (lactate, pH, pCO2), serum K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, troponin, FBC, coagulation screen
  - Pre-ROSC: none delay defibrillation
- **Imaging:**
  - Post-ROSC: portable CXR (line/tube placement, pneumothorax), bedside echo (focused cardiac ultrasound for tamponade, LV function, wall motion if ROSC)
- **Special tests:** None in acute arrest
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - ALS algorithm – shockable (pVT/VF) vs non-shockable (asystole/PEA) based on rhythm + pulse
- **Never-miss test:** **Rhythm check on monitor/pads** AND **confirm absence of pulse** – omission can lead to shocking a perfusing rhythm (causing arrest) or withholding shocks from a shockable rhythm

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Follow ALS/cardiac arrest algorithm (Resuscitation Council UK)
  - Defibrillation: 150–200 J biphasic, 360 J monophasic – as soon as shockable rhythm identified, charge while compressions ongoing, give shock immediately after pause
  - Immediately resume CPR 30:2 for 2 minutes before next rhythm check
  - Give **adrenaline 1 mg IV** [check local policy for timing – typically after 2nd or 3rd shock] then every 3–5 minutes
  - If refractory after 3 shocks → **amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus** (diluted in 20 mL D5W given fast) then 150 mg if recurrence
  - Manage reversible causes (4 Hs: hypoxia, hypovolaemia, hypo/hyperkalaemia/metabolic, hypothermia; 4 Ts: tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis – coronary or pulmonary)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - If ROSC achieved → transfer to ICU / coronary care unit
  - Targeted temperature management (32–36°C for 24h)
  - Coronary angiography (urgent if STEMI suspected; all presumed ischaemic)
  - Treat underlying cause: electrolyte correction, revascularisation, ICD therapy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Secondary prevention: implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) if no reversible cause (NICE TA95)
  - Assess left ventricular function (echo before discharge)
  - Medication: beta-blocker, ACEi, statin if ischaemic
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A for arrest itself; post-ROSC → ICU orders per local protocol

### Drugs & Doses
- **Adrenaline 1 mg (1:10 000 solution) IV push every 3–5 minutes during arrest** – can give via IO if no IV access
- **Amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus** (dilute to 20 mL with glucose 5%) for refractory shockable rhythm; may give second dose **150 mg** IV if recurrent
- **Alternatives:** Lidocaine 100 mg IV (if amiodarone unavailable) – but amiodarone is first-line in UK
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** None in arrest (do not delay); amiodarone – caution in pre-existing thyroid disease (post-ROSC). Avoid concurrent QT-prolonging drugs (not immediate concern)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose change needed in arrest. Post-ROSC: amiodarone metabolised by liver – reduce if severe hepatic impairment [check local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving amiodarone to a patient with unstable VT who has a pulse but is NOT in cardiac arrest** – can cause hypotension and precipitate arrest. Use synchronized cardioversion instead. Also: **delaying adrenaline until after first shock** is correct (not before)

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Failure to achieve ROSC
  - Post-arrest syndrome (myocardial stunning, systemic inflammation, brain injury)
  - Aspiration, rib fractures, pneumothorax from CPR
  - Amiodarone: hypotension (especially if given fast)
- **Chronic:**
  - Hypoxic brain injury (range: full recovery to persistent vegetative state)
  - Heart failure if myocardial infarction caused arrest
  - Need for ICD with its own complications (inappropriate shocks, infection)

### Prognosis
- Survival to hospital discharge for out-of-hospital shockable arrest ~20–30% if bystander CPR and early defibrillation; in-hospital witnessed arrest ~50%. Key predictors: time to defibrillation, initial rhythm, arrest cause (ischaemic vs non-ischaemic)

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Minimise pre-shock pause** – charge while compressions continue; give shock within 5 seconds of stopping chest compressions
- **Capnography waveform** – after ROSC, sudden rise in ETCO2 to >40 mmHg is a good sign
- **Consider reversible causes** aloud during arrest (e.g., hyperkalaemia, toxin – give calcium gluconate, sodium bicarbonate per local protocols; tension pneumothorax – needle decompression)

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do NOT delay defibrillation for IV access, intubation, or drug administration – shock first
- Do NOT give adrenaline before first shock (if local algorithm says after 2nd shock, wait)
- Do NOT stop chest compressions for longer than 10 seconds at any point (rhythm check, pulse check, shock)

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- Rare – most patients are not discharged directly from arrest. If discharged home after ROSC and ICD placement:
  - **Return if:** any shock from ICD (especially multiple or inappropriate), syncope, palpitations, chest pain
  - **Follow-up:** electrophysiology clinic within 4–6 weeks, cardiac rehabilitation, echo at 3 months

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call crash team** immediately on any pulseless, unresponsive patient (cardiac arrest call)
- **Post-ROSC escalation to ICU:** any ROSC >2 minutes, GCS <15, hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg despite fluids/vasopressors), lactate >4 mmol/L, or need for respiratory support
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 >7 (if not in arrest), sustained VF/pVT after 3 shocks → ask for senior anaesthetist/ICU consultant, consider alternative defibrillation (e.g., double sequential defibrillation per local protocol)
